title: Same Revenue, Different Price: The Revenue Quality Test Buyers Run Before They Talk Multiple
linkedin:
Two businesses. Same revenue. Same profit margin. Both trading for close to a decade.

One sells at four times earnings. The other struggles to clear two and a half.

The gap isn't in the P&L. It's in where the revenue actually came from.

We see this constantly when owner-led businesses start preparing for sale. Revenue is revenue on a profit and loss statement. It is not revenue on a buyer's model, where every dollar gets sorted by how confidently it repeats next year without the seller in the room.

Contracted or subscription income reads like a bond. A buyer can verify the terms, the renewal mechanics, the escalation clauses. Project-won or tender-won income, however reliable it has been in practice, reads like an option. Valuable, but only if the next round lands the way the last few did.

The test costs nothing to run. Take last year's revenue and split it into two columns: contracted and documented, versus actively re-won through a quote or tender. Most owners have never done this split, and the result is usually more lopsided than expected.

The number matters less than the direction it points. Businesses that move on this twelve to twenty four months before sale negotiate from strength. The ones that don't get the finding handed to them by a buyer's due diligence team instead.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#BusinessValuation #FractionalCFO #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Quick thought experiment: two businesses, same revenue, same margin, same years trading. One gets offered four times earnings. The other struggles past two and a half.

The difference isn't in the numbers you'd normally look at. It's whether that revenue is locked in with a contract or has to be re-won every year through quotes and tenders.

Have you ever actually split your own revenue into those two buckets? Most business owners we talk to haven't, and it's usually a bit more lopsided than they'd guess.

#BusinessOwners
google:
A question worth sitting with if you own a business on the East Coast: if you split last year's revenue into two piles, contracted or subscribed income on one side, and everything you had to actively re-win through quotes or tenders on the other, which pile would be bigger?

It matters more than most owners realise. Two businesses with identical revenue and profit can attract very different offers when they eventually go to market, and the difference usually comes down to that split. Contracted income reads as dependable to a buyer. Re-won income reads as a bet on next year going the way this year did.

The good news is this is fixable with enough lead time. Moving key clients from informal arrangements to documented multi-year agreements rarely changes the relationship itself. It changes what a buyer is allowed to assume about it.

If you're weighing up a sale in the next few years, or you're simply curious what your own revenue mix would tell a buyer, this week's blog post walks through the test. Read the full piece on the blog.
